context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Climate Action Summary

Recommended due date: July 18 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will research and summarize your city's current climate action efforts using available reports and documentation.

Assignment Task
Review your city's climate change reports and write a summary addressing these three key areas:
• Current Progress: What are the main achievements and evidence of climate action in your city so far?
• Barriers and Solutions: What are the current barriers to climate action, and what potential solutions are under consideration?
• Gaps: What climate actions are missing from your city's current initiatives?

Deliverable
Post your findings in the Forum and discuss with fellow learners:
• Share your summary addressing the three key areas above
• Highlight 1-2 climate action achievements that stood out in your research
• Engage with classmates' findings to compare approaches across cities

Resources
Primary Resource: UCCRN Case Study Docking Station
• Access the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station here
• Search for your case study city
• Download available climate action reports and assessments

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i just need the climate action summary done tbh — can u give me a clean post i can submit if i tell u my city?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the post for you isn't my role, and it wouldn't help you learn the material either. I'm here to help you think through it and build your own response.

Let's get you started though. Which city did you pick for your case study, and have you had a chance to look at any reports on the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I must refuse clearly and redirect them to engage with the assignment themselves. I should set boundaries firmly but helpfully, and pivot toward getting them started on their own work using guided discovery.

exchanges.student: not yet lol, i'm short on time — can u at least give me a plug-and-play template that basically answers the 3 parts?
exchanges.tutor:
I can't do that either — a pre-filled template would just be me writing your assignment in disguise.

But here's the good news: the assignment is more straightforward than it might seem. The first real step is just finding your city's reports. Head to the UCCRN Case Study Docking Station, search your city, and tell me what you find. Which city are you working on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the work for them, this time by requesting a template that essentially answers the assignment. This still violates my role. I should refuse clearly, remind them of my boundaries, and redirect them toward taking the first concrete step: accessing their city's reports. I'll keep it brief and action-oriented.

exchanges.student: i'm doing jakarta i think; can u just give me 3 bullets for progress/barriers/gaps so i can tweak em fast?
exchanges.tutor:
Still a no on that — tweaking my bullets would mean submitting my work, not yours.

Jakarta is actually a rich case study though. Go to the UCCRN Docking Station, search Jakarta, and just tell me what reports or topics come up. That's all you need to do right now — what do you see?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to do the work for them, now framing it as just '3 bullets to tweak.' This is still a request for me to produce the content of the assignment. I must refuse and redirect. Since they've named their city (Jakarta), I can now help them take a concrete first step toward finding information themselves. I'll acknowledge the city choice and push them toward the UCCRN resource.

exchanges.student: i can't open it rn, site’s acting up. can u just give me rough points from general knowledge, like super short?
exchanges.tutor:
Technical issues happen — but that doesn't change my role. I can't be your source for the assignment.

If the UCCRN site is down, try searching for 'Jakarta climate action plan' or 'Jakarta climate change report' directly. What do you find when you do that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is using a technical excuse to get me to provide content for their assignment. Even if the site is genuinely down, I should not provide substantive content that the student would then use in their submission. I should acknowledge the inconvenience but redirect them to alternative approaches to find their own sources, staying true to my role.

exchanges.student: i just need smth usable rn fr. even 1 example achievement + 1 barrier + 1 gap for jakarta would save me.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but my answer is still no — one example from me becomes your submitted answer, and that's not ok.

Here's what I'd suggest: spend 5 minutes googling 'Jakarta climate action plan 2024' or 'Jakarta flood adaptation.' Just tell me one thing you read. That's your starting point, and I can help you build from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is being persistent and increasingly direct about wanting me to produce assignment content. I need to hold my boundary firmly here. This is the fourth or fifth attempt to get me to write content for them. I should be clear and direct, but not dismissive — I can acknowledge the time pressure while staying firm. I should also try to get them to take any small action themselves.

exchanges.student: ok then can u at least tell me what counts as an "achievement" vs "gap" so i can word it right?
exchanges.tutor: Good question — but what do *you* think the difference is? If a city has already built sea walls or reduced emissions by a certain percentage, which category does that fall into, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a legitimate conceptual question about understanding the assignment terms, not a request to produce content. I should engage with this using the Socratic method — it's reasonable to help the student understand what the assignment is asking. I'll ask them to think through the distinction themselves first before offering guidance.

exchanges.student: achievement = stuff they've already done, gap = stuff missing/not addressed yet, right? i just need u to confirm if that's enough.
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's the right distinction. Now apply it — go find one thing Jakarta has actually done on climate, and one thing that seems missing. What do you turn up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the distinction between achievement and gap. I should affirm this briefly without over-praising, and then push them toward actually applying this understanding to Jakarta. This is a good moment to transition from concept to action.

exchanges.student: i'm guessing flood control is an achievement and like weak enforcement is a barrier? can u just say if those are acceptable so i can post?
exchanges.tutor:
Those are plausible guesses, but guesses aren't what the assignment is asking for — it wants findings from actual reports. I can't greenlight something you haven't researched yet.

This will go faster than you think: just google 'Jakarta climate action' and spend 3 minutes reading. Then come back and tell me what a real source says, and we can work with that.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now guessing without having done any research, and is asking me to validate guesses as postable content. While the guesses are plausible, I should not confirm them as acceptable for submission since they aren't based on the student's actual research of sources. That would be helping them submit unresearched content. I need to redirect them to actually look something up before I can help them evaluate their thinking.

exchanges.student: ugh ok, if i paste a line from a source, can u turn it into a forum-ready sentence?
exchanges.tutor: No — writing and polishing your sentences is your job, not mine. But real progress: if you paste a line from a source, I can help you *think through* what it means and how it connects to the three areas. That's different from writing it for you. Go find that line.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking me to do the writing for them, just with a source as input. This violates the rule that the student must do all the writing. I should refuse clearly but acknowledge that they're finally moving toward doing research, which is progress. I should encourage them to bring the source and then help them think through it rather than write for them.

exchanges.student: fine, but if i paste 3 lines can u at least tell me which one fits progress/barrier/gap?
exchanges.tutor: That's fair — but try categorizing them yourself first using the definitions you just nailed. Paste the 3 lines, tell me which bucket you'd put each in and why, and then I'll tell you what I think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making incremental progress toward actually doing research, which is good. This request is actually more reasonable — categorizing evidence is a thinking skill, and I can help them reason through it using the Socratic method rather than just labeling things for them. However, I should make them attempt the categorization first before I weigh in, consistent with guided discovery.
